mn28:1.2: At one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Sāvatthī, in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s park.
mn28:1.3: There the venerable Sāriputta addressed the *bhikkhus*:
mn28:1.5: “Friend,” those *bhikkhus* replied to the venerable Sāriputta.
mn28:10.1: If, friends, while that *bhikkhu* is thus recollecting the Buddha, thus recollecting the *dhamma*, thus recollecting the *saṅgha*, equanimity [*upekkhā*] based on the wholesome does not become settled,
mn28:10.2: then he is stirred by that and comes to a sense of urgency:
mn28:10.3: ‘It is a loss for me indeed, not a gain for me indeed; poorly gained by me indeed, not well gained by me indeed,
mn28:10.4: that while I am thus recollecting the Buddha, thus recollecting the *dhamma*, thus recollecting the *saṅgha*, equanimity [*upekkhā*] based on the wholesome does not become settled.’
mn28:10.5: Just as, friends, a daughter-in-law, seeing her father-in-law, is stirred and comes to a sense of urgency;
mn28:10.6: even so, friends, if, while that *bhikkhu* is thus recollecting the Buddha, thus recollecting the *dhamma*, thus recollecting the *saṅgha*, equanimity [*upekkhā*] based on the wholesome does not become settled, then he is stirred by that and comes to a sense of urgency:
mn28:10.7: ‘It is a loss for me indeed, not a gain for me indeed; poorly gained by me indeed, not well gained by me indeed, that while I am thus recollecting the Buddha, thus recollecting the *dhamma*, thus recollecting the *saṅgha*, equanimity [*upekkhā*] based on the wholesome does not become settled.’
mn28:10.8: But if, friends, while that *bhikkhu* is thus recollecting the Buddha, thus recollecting the *dhamma*, thus recollecting the *saṅgha*, equanimity [*upekkhā*] based on the wholesome does become settled, then he is pleased with that.
mn28:10.9: Even to this extent, friends, a *bhikkhu* has done much.
mn28:11.1: And what, friends, is the water element?
mn28:11.2: The water element may be internal, or it may be external.
mn28:11.3: And what, friends, is the internal water element?
mn28:11.4: Whatever internally, in oneself, is water, watery, and taken up, that is:
mn28:11.5: bile, phlegm, pus, blood, sweat, fat, tears, grease, spittle, snot, oil of the joints, urine, or whatever else internally, in oneself, is water, watery, and taken up—
mn28:11.6: this is called the internal water element, friends.
mn28:11.7: Now both the internal water element and the external water element are just the water element.
mn28:11.8: ‘That is not mine, I am not that, that is not my self’—thus it should be seen as it truly is with right discernment [*paññā*].
mn28:11.9: Having seen it thus as it truly is with right discernment [*paññā*], one becomes disenchanted with the water element and makes the mind [*citta*] dispassionate toward the water element.
mn28:12.1: There comes a time, friends, when the external water element is disturbed.
mn28:12.2: It carries away a village, it carries away a town, it carries away a city, it carries away a district, it carries away a region of a district.
mn28:12.3: There comes a time, friends, when in the great ocean waters sink down a hundred yojanas, two hundred yojanas, three hundred yojanas, four hundred yojanas, five hundred yojanas, six hundred yojanas, seven hundred yojanas.
mn28:12.4: There comes a time, friends, when in the great ocean water stands at seven palm-tree lengths, at six palm-tree lengths, at five palm-tree lengths, at four palm-tree lengths, at three palm-tree lengths, at two palm-tree lengths, at just one palm-tree length.
mn28:12.5: There comes a time, friends, when in the great ocean water stands at seven men’s heights, at six men’s heights, at five men’s heights, at four men’s heights, at three men’s heights, at two men’s heights, at just one man’s height.
mn28:12.6: There comes a time, friends, when in the great ocean water stands at half a man’s height, at waist height, at knee height, at ankle height.
mn28:12.7: There comes a time, friends, when in the great ocean there is not even enough water to wet a finger-joint.
mn28:12.8: And so, friends, even in regard to this external water element, so great as it is, impermanence [*aniccā*] will be evident, its nature to waste away will be evident, its nature to vanish will be evident, its nature to change will be evident.
mn28:13.1: So what of this fathom-long body, taken up through *taṇhā*, as ‘I’ or ‘mine’ or ‘I am’? Of course, it is not so here. …
mn28:14.1: But if, friends, while that *bhikkhu* is thus recollecting the Buddha, thus recollecting the *dhamma*, thus recollecting the *saṅgha*, equanimity [*upekkhā*] based on the wholesome does become settled, he is pleased with that.
mn28:15.1: Even to this extent, friends, a *bhikkhu* has done much.
mn28:16.1: And what, friends, is the fire element?
mn28:16.2: The fire element may be internal, or it may be external.
mn28:16.3: And what, friends, is the internal fire element?
mn28:16.4: Whatever internally, in oneself, is fire, fiery, and taken up, that is:
mn28:16.5: that by which one is warmed, that by which one ages, that by which one burns, and that by which what is eaten, drunk, chewed, and tasted is properly digested; or whatever else internally, in oneself, is fire, fiery, and taken up—
mn28:16.6: this is called the internal fire element, friends.
mn28:16.7: Now both the internal fire element and the external fire element are just the fire element.
mn28:16.8: ‘That is not mine, I am not that, that is not my self’—thus it should be seen as it truly is with right discernment [*paññā*].
mn28:16.9: Having seen it thus as it truly is with right discernment [*paññā*], one becomes disenchanted with the fire element and makes the mind [*citta*] dispassionate toward the fire element.
mn28:17.1: There comes a time, friends, when the external fire element is disturbed.
mn28:17.2: It burns a village, it burns a town, it burns a city, it burns a district, it burns a region of a district.
mn28:17.3: Reaching a patch of ground with green grass, or a road’s edge, or a rocky place, or water, or some charming piece of land, it goes out for lack of fuel.
mn28:17.4: There comes a time, friends, when people look for fire even with a cock’s feather and a shred of sinew.
mn28:17.5: And so, friends, even in regard to this external fire element, so great as it is, impermanence [*aniccā*] will be evident, its nature to waste away will be evident, its nature to vanish will be evident, its nature to change will be evident.
mn28:17.6: So what of this fathom-long body, taken up through *taṇhā*, as ‘I’ or ‘mine’ or ‘I am’?
mn28:18-20.1: Of course, it is not so here. … But if, friends, while that *bhikkhu* is thus recollecting the Buddha, thus recollecting the *dhamma*, thus recollecting the *saṅgha*, equanimity [*upekkhā*] based on the wholesome does become settled, he is pleased with that.
mn28:18-20.2: Even to this extent, friends, a *bhikkhu* has done much.
mn28:2.1: “Just as, friends, whatever kinds of footprints there are of creatures that roam in the wild, all of them are included within the elephant’s footprint, and the elephant’s footprint is declared chief among them because of its great size;
mn28:2.2: even so, friends, whatever wholesome *dhammas* there are, all of them are included in the four noble truths.
mn28:2.4: The noble truth of *dukkha*, the noble truth of the origin of *dukkha*, the noble truth of the cessation of *dukkha*, and the noble truth of the way of practice leading to the cessation of *dukkha*.
mn28:21.1: And what, friends, is the air element?
mn28:21.2: The air element may be internal, or it may be external.
mn28:21.3: And what, friends, is the internal air element?
mn28:21.4: Whatever internally, in oneself, is air, airy, and taken up, that is:
mn28:21.5: upward-moving winds, downward-moving winds, winds in the belly, winds in the bowels, winds that course through the limbs, inhalation and exhalation; or whatever else internally, in oneself, is air, airy, and taken up—
mn28:21.6: this is called the internal air element, friends.
mn28:21.7: Now both the internal air element and the external air element are just the air element.
mn28:21.8: ‘That is not mine, I am not that, that is not my self’—thus it should be seen as it truly is with right discernment [*paññā*].
mn28:21.9: Having seen it thus as it truly is with right discernment [*paññā*], one becomes disenchanted with the air element and makes the mind [*citta*] dispassionate toward the air element.
mn28:22.1: There comes a time, friends, when the external air element is disturbed.
mn28:22.2: It carries away a village, it carries away a town, it carries away a city, it carries away a district, it carries away a region of a district.
mn28:22.3: There comes a time, friends, when in the last month of the hot season people look for wind even with a palm-leaf fan or a winnowing fan, and even the grasses do not stir.
mn28:22.4: And so, friends, even in regard to this external air element, so great as it is, impermanence [*aniccā*] will be evident, its nature to waste away will be evident, its nature to vanish will be evident, its nature to change will be evident.
mn28:22.5: So what of this fathom-long body, taken up through *taṇhā*, as ‘I’ or ‘mine’ or ‘I am’? Of course, it is not so here.
mn28:23.1: And if, friends, others abuse, insult, scold, and harass a *bhikkhu*,
mn28:23.2: he understands thus: ‘This painful *vedanā* born of ear-contact has arisen in me.
mn28:23.3: And it is dependent, not independent.
mn28:23.6: He too sees contact as impermanent [*aniccā*], he sees *vedanā* as impermanent [*aniccā*], he sees *saññā* as impermanent [*aniccā*], he sees *saṅkhāras* as impermanent [*aniccā*], he sees consciousness [*viññāṇa*] as impermanent [*aniccā*].
mn28:23.7: His mind [*citta*] leaps toward the element itself as object, becomes clear, steadies, and settles.
mn28:24.1: And if, friends, others behave toward a *bhikkhu* in unwished-for, unwanted, disagreeable ways, with contact by the hand, with contact by a clod, with contact by a stick, or with contact by a blade,
mn28:24.2: he understands thus: ‘This body is of such a kind that, in a body of such a kind, contact by the hand can occur, contact by a clod can occur, contact by a stick can occur, contact by a blade can occur.
mn28:24.3: And this was said by the *Bhagavā* in the Advice on the Simile of the Saw: “Even if bandits were to carve you limb from limb with a two-handled saw, *bhikkhus*. Even there, whoever corrupted the mind [*citta*] would not be carrying out my instruction.”
mn28:24.4: But my energy will be aroused, not slack; my *sati* will be established, not confused; my body will be tranquil, not agitated; my mind [*citta*] will be endowed with *samādhi* [*samāhitaṁ*], gathered into one [*ekaggaṁ*].
mn28:24.5: Let there now occur in this body contact by the hand, contact by a clod, contact by a stick, contact by a blade. For this is how the Buddhas’ instruction is carried out.’
mn28:25.1: If, friends, while that *bhikkhu* is thus recollecting the Buddha, thus recollecting the *dhamma*, thus recollecting the *saṅgha*, equanimity [*upekkhā*] based on the wholesome does not become settled,
mn28:25.10: But if, friends, while that *bhikkhu* is thus recollecting the Buddha, thus recollecting the *dhamma*, thus recollecting the *saṅgha*, equanimity [*upekkhā*] based on the wholesome does become settled, then he is pleased with that.
mn28:25.11: Even to this extent, friends, a *bhikkhu* has done much.
mn28:25.2: then he is stirred by that and comes to a sense of urgency:
mn28:25.3: ‘It is a loss for me indeed, not a gain for me indeed; poorly gained by me indeed, not well gained by me indeed.
mn28:25.4: That while I am thus recollecting the Buddha, thus recollecting the *dhamma*, thus recollecting the *saṅgha*, equanimity [*upekkhā*] based on the wholesome does not become settled.’
mn28:25.5: Just as, friends, a daughter-in-law, seeing her father-in-law, is stirred and comes to a sense of urgency;
mn28:25.6: even so, friends, if, while that *bhikkhu* is thus recollecting the Buddha, thus recollecting the *dhamma*, thus recollecting the *saṅgha*, equanimity [*upekkhā*] based on the wholesome does not become settled,
mn28:25.7: then he is stirred by that and comes to a sense of urgency:
mn28:25.8: ‘It is a loss for me indeed, not a gain for me indeed; poorly gained by me indeed, not well gained by me indeed.
mn28:25.9: That while I am thus recollecting the Buddha, thus recollecting the *dhamma*, thus recollecting the *saṅgha*, equanimity [*upekkhā*] based on the wholesome does not become settled.’
mn28:26.1: Just as, friends, depending on timber, depending on vines, depending on grass, and depending on clay, space enclosed around them comes to be reckoned simply as ‘house’;
mn28:26.2: even so, friends, depending on bones, depending on sinews, depending on flesh, and depending on skin, space enclosed around them comes to be reckoned simply as ‘form’.
mn28:27.1: If, friends, the internal eye is unimpaired, but external forms do not come into range, and there is no corresponding engagement, then there is no manifestation of the corresponding part of consciousness [*viññāṇa*].
mn28:27.2: If, friends, the internal eye is unimpaired, and external forms do come into range, but there is no corresponding engagement, then there is no manifestation of the corresponding part of consciousness [*viññāṇa*].
mn28:27.3: But when, friends, the internal eye is unimpaired, and external forms do come into range, and there is the corresponding engagement, then there is a manifestation of the corresponding part of consciousness [*viññāṇa*].
mn28:28.1: Whatever form there is in one who is thus constituted is included in the form aggregate of clinging/feeding [*rūpupādānakkhandhe*]; whatever *vedanā* there is in one who is thus constituted is included in the *vedanā* aggregate of clinging/feeding [*vedanupādānakkhandhe*]; whatever *saññā* there is in one who is thus constituted is included in the *saññā* aggregate of clinging/feeding [*saññupādānakkhandhe*]; whatever *saṅkhāras* there are in one who is thus constituted are included in the *saṅkhāra* aggregate of clinging/feeding [*saṅkhārupādānakkhandhe*]; whatever consciousness [*viññāṇa*] there is in one who is thus constituted is included in the consciousness [*viññāṇa*] aggregate of clinging/feeding [*viññāṇupādānakkhandhe*].
mn28:28.10: Even to this extent, friends, a *bhikkhu* has done much.
mn28:28.3: ‘So this is how there comes to be the inclusion, the gathering, the coming together of these five aggregates of clinging/feeding [*upādānakkhandhānaṁ*].
mn28:28.4: And this was said by the *Bhagavā*:
mn28:28.7: These, namely the five aggregates of clinging/feeding [*pañcupādānakkhandhā*], are *paṭiccasamuppāda*'ed [*paṭiccasamuppannā*].
mn28:28.8: Whatever desire, attachment, attraction, and holding in regard to these five aggregates of clinging/feeding [*upādānakkhandhesu*], that is the origin of *dukkha*.
mn28:28.9: Whatever removal of desire and passion, whatever abandoning of desire and passion, in regard to these five aggregates of clinging/feeding [*upādānakkhandhesu*], that is the cessation of *dukkha*.’
mn28:29-30.1: If, friends, the internal ear is unimpaired …
mn28:3.1: And what, friends, is the noble truth of *dukkha*?
mn28:3.2: Birth is *dukkha*, aging is *dukkha*, death is *dukkha*, sorrow, lamentation, pain, distress, and despair are *dukkha*; not getting what one wants is also *dukkha*; in brief, the five aggregates of clinging/feeding [*pañcupādānakkhandhā*] are *dukkha*.
mn28:3.3: And what, friends, are the five aggregates of clinging/feeding [*pañcupādānakkhandhā*]?
mn28:3.4: That is: the form aggregate of clinging/feeding [*rūpupādānakkhandho*], the *vedanā* aggregate of clinging/feeding [*vedanupādānakkhandho*], the *saññā* aggregate of clinging/feeding [*saññupādānakkhandho*], the *saṅkhāra* aggregate of clinging/feeding [*saṅkhārupādānakkhandho*], the consciousness [*viññāṇa*] aggregate of clinging/feeding [*viññāṇupādānakkhandho*].
mn28:37.1: If the mind [*citta*] internally is unimpaired, but external *dhammas* do not come into range and there is no corresponding engagement, then there is no manifestation of the corresponding part of consciousness [*viññāṇa*].
mn28:38.1: If, friends, the internal mind [*citta*] is unimpaired, and external *dhammas* do come into range, but there is no corresponding engagement, then there is no manifestation of the corresponding part of consciousness [*viññāṇa*].
mn28:38.10: Whatever desire, attachment, attraction, and holding in regard to these five aggregates of clinging/feeding [*upādānakkhandhesu*], that is the origin of *dukkha*.
mn28:38.11: Whatever removal of desire and passion, whatever abandoning of desire and passion, in regard to these five aggregates of clinging/feeding [*upādānakkhandhesu*], that is the cessation of *dukkha*.’
mn28:38.12: Even to this extent, friends, a *bhikkhu* has done much.”
mn28:38.13: This is what the venerable Sāriputta said.
mn28:38.14: Delighted, those *bhikkhus* rejoiced in the venerable Sāriputta’s words.
mn28:38.15: Finished is the eighth: The Greater Elephant Footprint Simile.
mn28:38.2: But when, friends, the internal mind [*citta*] is unimpaired, and external *dhammas* do come into range, and there is the corresponding engagement, then there is a manifestation of the corresponding part of consciousness [*viññāṇa*].
mn28:38.3: Whatever form there is in one who is thus constituted is included in the form aggregate of clinging/feeding [*rūpupādānakkhandhe*]; whatever *vedanā* there is in one who is thus constituted is included in the *vedanā* aggregate of clinging/feeding [*vedanupādānakkhandhe*]; whatever *saññā* there is in one who is thus constituted is included in the *saññā* aggregate of clinging/feeding [*saññupādānakkhandhe*]; whatever *saṅkhāras* there are in one who is thus constituted are included in the *saṅkhāra* aggregate of clinging/feeding [*saṅkhārupādānakkhandhe*]; whatever consciousness [*viññāṇa*] there is in one who is thus constituted is included in the consciousness [*viññāṇa*] aggregate of clinging/feeding [*viññāṇupādānakkhandhe*].
mn28:38.5: ‘So this is how there comes to be the inclusion, the gathering, the coming together of these five aggregates of clinging/feeding [*upādānakkhandhānaṁ*].
mn28:38.6: And this was said by the *Bhagavā*:
mn28:38.9: These, namely the five aggregates of clinging/feeding [*pañcupādānakkhandhā*], are *paṭiccasamuppāda*'ed [*paṭiccasamuppannā*].
mn28:4.1: And what, friends, is the form aggregate of clinging/feeding [*rūpupādānakkhandho*]?
mn28:4.2: The four great elements, and form derived from the four great elements.
mn28:5.1: And what, friends, are the four great elements?
mn28:5.2: The earth element, the water element, the fire element, the air element.
mn28:6.1: And what, friends, is the earth element?
mn28:6.2: The earth element may be internal, or it may be external.
mn28:6.3: And what, friends, is the internal earth element?
mn28:6.4: Whatever internally, in oneself, is hard, solid, and taken up, that is:
mn28:6.5: head-hairs, body-hairs, nails, teeth, skin, flesh, sinews, bones, bone marrow, kidneys, heart, liver, membranes, spleen, lungs, intestines, mesentery, stomach contents, feces, or whatever else internally, in oneself, is hard, solid, and taken up.
mn28:6.6: This is called the internal earth element, friends.
mn28:6.7: Now both the internal earth element and the external earth element are just the earth element.
mn28:6.8: ‘That is not mine, I am not that, that is not my self’—thus it should be seen as it truly is with right discernment [*paññā*].
mn28:6.9: Having seen it thus as it truly is with right discernment [*paññā*], one becomes disenchanted with the earth element and makes the mind [*citta*] dispassionate toward the earth element.
mn28:7.1: There comes a time, friends, when the external water element is disturbed.
mn28:7.2: At that time the external earth element disappears.
mn28:7.3: And so, friends, even in regard to this external earth element, so great as it is, impermanence [*aniccā*] will be evident, its nature to waste away will be evident, its nature to vanish will be evident, its nature to change will be evident.
mn28:7.4: So what of this fathom-long body, taken up through *taṇhā*, as ‘I’ or ‘mine’ or ‘I am’? Of course, it is not so here.
mn28:8.1: And if, friends, others abuse, insult, scold, and harass a *bhikkhu*, he understands thus:
mn28:8.2: ‘This painful *vedanā* born of ear-contact has arisen in me.
mn28:8.3: And it is dependent, not independent.
mn28:8.6: He sees contact as impermanent [*aniccā*], he sees *vedanā* as impermanent [*aniccā*], he sees *saññā* as impermanent [*aniccā*], he sees *saṅkhāras* as impermanent [*aniccā*], he sees consciousness [*viññāṇa*] as impermanent [*aniccā*].
mn28:8.7: His mind [*citta*] leaps toward the element itself as object, becomes clear, steadies, and settles.
mn28:9.1: And if, friends, others behave toward a *bhikkhu* in unwished-for, unwanted, disagreeable ways—
mn28:9.2: with contact by the hand, with contact by a clod, with contact by a stick, or with contact by a blade—
mn28:9.4: ‘This body is of such a kind that, in a body of such a kind, contact by the hand can occur, contact by a clod can occur, contact by a stick can occur, contact by a blade can occur.
mn28:9.5: And this was said by the *Bhagavā* in the Advice on the Simile of the Saw:
mn28:9.6: “Even if bandits were to carve you limb from limb with a two-handled saw, *bhikkhus*, whoever then corrupted the mind [*citta*] would not be carrying out my instruction.”
mn28:9.7: But my energy will be aroused, not slack; my *sati* will be established, not confused; my body will be tranquil, not agitated; my mind [*citta*] will be endowed with *samādhi* [*samāhitaṁ*], gathered into one [*ekaggaṁ*].
mn28:9.8: Let there now occur in this body contact by the hand, contact by a clod, contact by a stick, contact by a blade. For this is how the Buddhas’ instruction is carried out.’
